title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Space Tourism on Science

texts.A: [37]The burgeoning industry of space tourism is often sold as a gateway to the stars for all, but it is currently nothing more than a vanity project for the ultra-wealthy.[/37] [38]The environmental cost of a single rocket launch, in terms of carbon emissions and atmospheric soot, is astronomical compared to the benefit of a few minutes of weightlessness.[/38] [40]We should be focusing our aerospace engineering talent on climate-monitoring satellites and planetary defense, not on building luxury 'space hotels' that offer no scientific value and only serve to widen the gap between the elite and the rest of humanity.[/40]
texts.B: To dismiss space tourism as a 'vanity project' is to miss how innovation works. Every major leap in aviation, from the first commercial flights to the jet engine, was initially funded by the wealthy. The high ticket prices of today are the R&D budget for the affordable space travel of tomorrow. [39]Furthermore, the private sector's involvement has drastically reduced the cost per kilogram of launching payloads into orbit, which is a direct benefit to the scientific community.[/39] [37]Space tourism is the engine that will finally make the colonization of the solar system economically viable.[/37]
texts.C: [38]I share the concern about the immediate environmental impact of chemical rockets, but the solution is 'green' propulsion, not a ban on tourism.[/38] [40]However, I agree that the current focus on 'sub-orbital hops' is scientifically hollow.[/40] We need to ensure that every commercial flight carries a mandatory suite of autonomous experiments to study the upper atmosphere. If we treat every tourist craft as a mobile laboratory, we can turn a luxury experience into a massive data-gathering exercise. The 'Overview Effect'—the shift in perspective seen by astronauts—could also be a powerful tool for global environmental awareness.
texts.D: The real danger of the space tourism boom is the 'Kessler Syndrome'—the possibility that a collision between satellites or debris could create a cloud of junk that makes orbit unusable for generations. Adding thousands of 'tourist' craft to an already crowded low-Earth orbit is a recipe for disaster. [39]While I share the view that private investment is necessary for progress, I believe it must be strictly regulated by an international body.[/39] We cannot allow a 'Wild West' atmosphere in space where profit-seeking companies jeopardize the vital GPS and weather satellites that our modern civilization depends on.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the long-term justification for space tourism?
questions.explanation: Expert B justifies it as the 'R&D' for future travel and a way to make colonization viable. Expert A argues it is a 'vanity project' with 'no scientific value' and an unjustifiable environmental cost.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the environmental or atmospheric consequences of rocket launches?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions the 'environmental cost' and 'carbon emissions.' Expert C explicitly shares the 'concern about the immediate environmental impact of chemical rockets.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the role of private companies in advancing space technology?
questions.explanation: Expert B says private involvement has 'drastically reduced' costs. Expert D also 'shares the view that private investment is necessary for progress,' though they emphasize the need for regulation.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether commercial space flights can be considered scientifically useful in their current form?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes they are 'scientifically hollow' in their current sub-orbital form (unless changes are made). Expert A agrees, stating they offer 'no scientific value' at all.
